reasoning: Both reviewers independently converge on the same fundamental conclusion: this manuscript presents strong quantitative claims (20% IoU improvement, 30% processing time reduction, superior robustness) without providing any of the necessary supporting evidence. There are no actual numerical results in tables or figures, no dataset specification, no description of the proposed methodological modifications, no ground truth annotation procedure, no baseline configuration, and no statistical analysis. The manuscript also contains placeholder text ('[JOURNAL]'), incomplete sentences, and dubious references, suggesting it is either an unfinished draft or generated by a template/AI tool without actual experiments having been conducted. The central conclusions are fundamentally unsupported by the data as presented—not merely improvable, but entirely absent in terms of evidentiary backing.

findings.finding: No actual experimental results are reported. The claimed quantitative improvements (20% IoU, 30% speed reduction) appear only as summary assertions in the text with no supporting tables, figures, confidence intervals, p-values, or any analyzable data.
findings.justification: The paper's central conclusions rest entirely on quantitative performance claims, yet no numerical evidence is provided anywhere in the manuscript. This is not a matter of incomplete reporting—the evidence is wholly absent, rendering the conclusions baseless.

findings.finding: No dataset is identified or described. There are no dataset names, subject counts, number of images/videos, train/test splits, annotation procedures, or acquisition protocols.
findings.justification: Without a defined evaluation dataset and ground truth, the reported accuracy and efficiency improvements cannot be interpreted, reproduced, or verified. The empirical foundation of the paper does not exist.

findings.finding: The proposed methodological contribution is never described. The paper claims 'novel modifications,' 'refined algorithms,' and 'advanced neural network architectures' integrated into MediaPipe but provides no algorithmic details, architectural changes, code modifications, or technical description of what was actually done.
findings.justification: A paper claiming methodological innovation must describe the method. Without any concrete description of the purported enhancements, there is nothing to evaluate, reproduce, or connect causally to the claimed performance gains. The scientific contribution is unverifiable.

findings.finding: Evaluation metrics are inappropriate or inadequately defined for human pose estimation. IoU and MSE are used without defining what is being compared (keypoints, bounding boxes, skeleton masks), and standard pose estimation metrics (PCK, OKS/mAP, MPJPE) are absent.
findings.justification: The 20% IoU improvement headline claim is uninterpretable because it is unclear what IoU measures in this context. If the metric does not correspond to the actual task, the central accuracy claim is meaningless even if numbers were provided.

findings.finding: No ground truth measurement procedure is described. It is unclear how reference pose labels were obtained for accuracy evaluation.
findings.justification: Accuracy metrics require reference labels. Without knowing the source and quality of ground truth, reported accuracy improvements are unvalidatable. Combined with the absence of dataset details, this confirms the empirical claims are unsupported.

findings.finding: Computational efficiency claims (30% processing time reduction) are reported without hardware specifications, software environment, image resolution, latency measurement protocol, or any experimental details.
findings.justification: Runtime performance is entirely dependent on measurement conditions. Without these details, the efficiency claim cannot be interpreted or reproduced and is therefore unsupported as presented.

findings.finding: The manuscript contains placeholder text ('[JOURNAL]'), incomplete sentences, duplicated paragraphs, and references with fabricated-seeming author names ('Developer, B.', 'Researcher, A.'), indicating the paper is either an unfinished draft or generated by a template/AI tool.
findings.justification: While this could reflect OCR damage or a draft state rather than scientific fraud, it means the manuscript cannot be assessed as a complete scientific work. This compounds the evidentiary problems but is categorized as MAJOR-REVISION because it pertains to completeness and editorial quality rather than a specific broken scientific claim.

findings.finding: No training or fine-tuning procedure is reported despite claims of integrating advanced neural network architectures.
findings.justification: If the system was retrained, the absence of optimization details makes results non-reproducible. If no training occurred, the architectural enhancement claims are misleading. This is a serious gap but is subsumed by the broader absence of methodology.

findings.finding: The baseline comparison is undefined. No model versions, parameter settings, hardware, or fairness conditions for the comparison are specified.
findings.justification: Without a properly defined baseline, claimed improvements could reflect unfair comparisons rather than genuine gains. This could materially change conclusions but is secondary to the complete absence of results data.

findings.finding: Claims about robustness under low light and occlusion are unsupported by any stratified experiment, condition definitions, or per-condition results.
findings.justification: These specific claims could change substantially with proper testing. The issue undermines robustness conclusions specifically.

findings.finding: The manuscript conflates general pose estimation benchmarking with a trivial curl-counter application (angle thresholding), which is neither novel nor validated.
findings.justification: Presenting a simple heuristic application as evidence of generalized pose estimation improvement overstates the contribution. The curl counter is not evaluated for accuracy or robustness.

findings.finding: Multi-person pose estimation is claimed but no dedicated evaluation protocol or metrics are provided.
findings.justification: If multi-person capability is a claimed contribution, its absence from evaluation undermines that specific claim.

findings.finding: No external validation or holdout testing is described, creating risk of overfitting or optimistic reporting.
findings.justification: Without separation between development and evaluation data, reported gains may not generalize. Moot given the broader absence of data, but relevant if the paper is revised.

findings.finding: References are dubious, incomplete, or contain placeholder entries, weakening the literature grounding.
findings.justification: Poor referencing reduces credibility but does not itself invalidate empirical claims.

review_a.reasoning: The manuscript lacks the methodological detail and evidentiary reporting required to support its central performance claims. The paper asserts substantial improvements (e.g., 20% IoU gain, 30% processing-time reduction, superior low-light/occlusion performance, benchmarking against traditional models), but provides no usable description of datasets, sample sizes, ground truth annotation procedure, baseline configuration, training/fine-tuning protocol, statistical results, or even the actual contents of the referenced tables/figures. In addition, the reported evaluation metrics appear mismatched or undefined for the claimed task. As presented, the main conclusions are not reproducibly or verifiably supported.

findings.finding: Core experimental evidence is missing: no actual numerical results are reported in Table 1 or the cited figures, despite strong claims of improved accuracy and efficiency.
findings.justification: The paper’s main conclusions depend on quantitative comparisons, but the manuscript provides no analyzable result tables, no confidence intervals, no p-values, no variances, and no concrete benchmark numbers except isolated headline percentages. Without the underlying results, the central claims cannot be supported by the data as presented.

findings.finding: No dataset is identified with sufficient specificity: no dataset names, no subject counts, no number of videos/images, no train/validation/test split, no annotation source, and no acquisition protocol details.
findings.justification: Claims of accuracy improvement in pose estimation require evaluation against known ground truth on a defined dataset. Without a defined dataset and split, the reported performance cannot be interpreted, reproduced, or trusted; the central empirical conclusions are unsupported.

findings.finding: Evaluation metrics are inappropriate or inadequately defined for the stated task. The manuscript uses MSE and IoU for pose estimation without specifying what objects/keypoints are being compared, how IoU is computed for pose outputs, or what target variable Y represents.
findings.justification: Human pose estimation is typically evaluated using keypoint-based metrics (e.g., PCK, OKS/mAP, MPJPE for 3D). IoU is not a standard standalone metric for landmark pose estimation unless one defines derived regions/skeleton masks or detection boxes. Because the paper does not define the prediction target or how metrics map to pose landmarks, the claimed 20% IoU improvement is not interpretable and may not measure the intended task at all.

findings.finding: The baseline comparison is undefined. The manuscript states that the optimized MediaPipe framework is benchmarked against traditional models/baseline MediaPipe, but does not specify model versions, parameter settings, hardware, implementation details, or whether comparisons are fair.
findings.justification: If the baseline is weak, outdated, or run under different conditions, the claimed gains could change substantially. This does not prove the conclusions are false, but it makes them unreliable pending proper comparative methodology.

findings.finding: The proposed methodological contribution is not actually described. The paper repeatedly mentions 'novel modifications', 'refined algorithms', and 'advanced neural network architectures' integrated into MediaPipe, but does not specify what was changed, how, or why.
findings.justification: A paper claiming methodological improvements must describe the method. Without a concrete algorithmic description, neither reproducibility nor scientific evaluation is possible, and the asserted causal link between modifications and performance gains cannot be assessed.

findings.finding: No training/fine-tuning procedure is reported, despite claims of integrating advanced neural network architectures and optimizing the framework.
findings.justification: If the system was retrained or fine-tuned, the absence of optimization details (losses, epochs, learning rate, augmentation, initialization) makes the results non-reproducible and could materially affect conclusions. If no training occurred, then claims about architectural enhancement are misleading. Clarification is essential.

findings.finding: No ground-truth measurement procedure is described for accuracy estimation. It is unclear how true pose labels were obtained for walking/running scenes in controlled and natural settings.
findings.justification: Accuracy metrics such as MSE require reference labels. Without a description of the source and quality of ground truth, accuracy claims cannot be validated; the main performance conclusion is unsupported.

findings.finding: The statistical analysis is asserted but not actually performed or reported. The manuscript mentions 'advanced statistical methods' yet provides only formulas for MSE and IoU, with no inferential analysis, uncertainty quantification, or repeated-run variability.
findings.justification: Lack of uncertainty reporting means the claimed gains may reflect noise, hardware variance, or sample composition. This weakens confidence and could alter conclusions, though it does not automatically invalidate all results if raw comparisons were otherwise sound.

findings.finding: The manuscript conflates at least two distinct tasks: general human pose estimation benchmarking and a curl-counter application based on a single joint angle thresholding heuristic.
findings.justification: A biceps-curl counter is not evidence of generalized pose-estimation improvement. If application-specific logic is presented as validation of overall pose estimation gains, conclusions may be overstated. The core claim may survive, but task separation and evaluation redesign are needed.

findings.finding: The angle computation and stage-classification heuristic are presented, but no validation of this subsystem is given (e.g., counting accuracy, tolerance to viewpoint, subject variability, or threshold sensitivity).
findings.justification: This weakens the exercise-analysis application section, but does not by itself negate the broader pose-estimation claims.

findings.finding: The manuscript claims both 'multi-person pose estimation capabilities' and shows a system architecture, but provides no multi-person evaluation protocol or metrics.
findings.justification: If multi-person capability is part of the claimed contribution, the absence of dedicated evaluation could materially affect the scope and validity of the conclusions.

findings.finding: Computational-efficiency claims are unsupported by required experimental details: no hardware specs, software environment, image resolution, batch size/streaming mode, or latency measurement protocol are reported.
findings.justification: A 30% reduction in processing time cannot be interpreted without measurement conditions. Runtime is hardware- and resolution-dependent; absent these details, the central efficiency claim is not supportable from the manuscript as presented.

findings.finding: The manuscript makes strong claims about robustness under low light and occlusion, but no stratified experiment, condition definitions, or per-condition results are provided.
findings.justification: These claims could change substantially with proper testing. The issue undermines specific robustness conclusions, though not necessarily all claims if general improvements were otherwise demonstrated.

findings.finding: There is no evidence of external validation or holdout testing distinct from the data used for optimization, creating a risk of overfitting or optimistic reporting.
findings.justification: Without a clear separation between development and evaluation data, reported gains may not generalize. This could change conclusions but does not prove invalidity on its own.

findings.finding: The manuscript contains signs of severe editorial corruption or placeholder text ([JOURNAL], incomplete references, duplicated/reordered passages, abrupt sentence breaks), raising concern that the methods/results sections may be incomplete or malformed.
findings.justification: This may reflect OCR damage rather than scientific error, but if the manuscript itself is in this state, reviewers cannot reliably assess methodology. The paper requires substantial reconstruction before valid review of its claims.

findings.finding: Several references appear dubious, incomplete, or nonstandard for the cited claims (e.g., placeholder authors such as 'Developer, B.' and 'Researcher, A.', incomplete venue information), weakening the literature grounding.
findings.justification: Poor referencing does not itself invalidate the empirical claims, but it reduces credibility and makes it difficult to verify contextual assertions.

review_b.reasoning: The manuscript presents itself as a completed research study with quantitative results and code, but it is not a proper research paper. It lacks all necessary methodological details (no dataset description, no experimental setup, no baseline comparison method, no statistical rigour), contains placeholder journal references, and presents fabricated-seeming numerical results (20% IoU improvement, 30% speed improvement) without any supporting evidence, data, or analysis. The paper reads as a generic, AI-generated template with no actual experiments, making it impossible to evaluate or reproduce. This constitutes a fundamental lack of scientific validity.

findings.finding: The manuscript contains no description of a dataset used for evaluation. It mentions 'high-resolution cameras' and 'systematic recording' but provides no specifics on subjects, number of frames, types of activities, annotation methods, or train/test splits. Without a dataset, any reported performance numbers are meaningless.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the central quantitative claims (20% IoU improvement, 30% speed improvement) cannot be attributed to any reproducible experiment. The paper's conclusions are unsupported.

findings.finding: The quantitative results (20% IoU improvement, 30% processing time reduction) are stated as summary claims in the conclusion without any supporting tables, figures, or statistical analysis. No baseline model is explicitly named or compared against. No confidence intervals, error bars, or test conditions are reported.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the paper's primary quantitative findings are unsubstantiated. No data is presented to back them up, making the conclusions baseless.

findings.finding: The paper claims 'improvements to the MediaPipe framework' but provides no algorithmic details, architectural diagrams, or code modifications. The only technical formula given is a basic angle calculation, which is standard and not a novel contribution. The GitHub link leads to a generic repository that, even if functional, is not described as implementing the claimed optimizations.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the paper does not actually describe or deliver the purported technical innovation. There is no method to evaluate or reproduce.

findings.finding: The manuscript is incomplete and contains placeholder text. Examples: missing text after 'early methods relied heavily on hand-crafted features and classical machine learning' (cut off mid-sentence), repeated 'The enhancements addressed existing lacunae...' paragraph, 'consider the ethical implications of enhanced human' cut off, and multiple '[JOURNAL]' placeholders in references. This indicates the paper is not a finished, submitted manuscript.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the manuscript is either an unfinished draft or generated by a template/generative AI without proper curation. It cannot be reviewed as a complete scientific work.

findings.finding: The paper states 'this study employs an experimental research design' but then provides no experimental protocol. There is no description of the computing hardware, software versions, hyperparameters, training procedure (if any), or evaluation protocol. Metrics like MSE are mentioned but never reported.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the claimed experimental evaluation is entirely absent. The study cannot be replicated or assessed for validity.

findings.finding: References are cited inconsistently and include placeholder entries (e.g., 'Developer, B., 2020' with a fake-sounding title, 'Researcher, A., 2021' with a generic title). Many references are incomplete (missing page numbers, journals). This suggests citation fabrication or sloppy assembly.
findings.justification: If confirmed, the scholarly foundation of the paper is compromised. Key citations may not exist, undermining the literature review and context.

findings.finding: The 'curl counter logic' is presented as a contribution but is a trivial application of angle thresholding with no novelty. The paper does not connect this to the claimed framework enhancements or evaluate its performance.
findings.justification: If confirmed, this does not affect the central conclusions because it is not a core claim of the paper, but it reflects a lack of substantive methodological contribution.
